返回本批次

厌倦了吃自己的狗粮？尝试闻自己的屁

Bored of eating your own dogfood? Try smelling your own farts

shkspr.mobi 作者 ColinWright 分数 344 评论 204 2026/03/22 21:12
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 前几天我给一家大公司打电话。我是否知道我想要的信息可以在他们的网站上找到？0 我是否知道我可以在线管理我的帐户？1 我是否希望收到通过 WhatsApp 与他们的人工智能助手聊天的链接？2 当然，通话量高于预期。我只能假设负责预测通话量的人最近遭受了脑外伤，并且无法在不将另一只手从基础上抽出的情况下数到五以上。那个开朗的女人颤抖着她预先录制的剧本，突然被一个可怕的电子怪物所取代。我把通话录音3，以便您能体验这座合成荣耀的丰碑！ 🔊💾 下载此音频文件。这是来自一家公司，该公司的网站上滔滔不绝地宣传其创新性。人工智能正在大规模改变其业务！致力于卓越技术并在其所有部门提供 ISO 认证的质量！然而，在某个地方，有人认为客户体验已经足够好了。 “Dogfooding”是科技行业的一种神圣做法。使用您自己的产品。就是这样。这就是你所要做的。例如，如果您在 Slack 工作，则无法使用 Teams 作为消息传递解决方案。您必须向人们表明您对自己的产品充满信心。但它的意义远不止于此。当我曾经在移动电话网络工作时，他们要求我们花时间在呼叫中心。仅仅收到有关客户 KPI 的季度报告是不够的。当客户与您的计费系统发生冲突时，您必须听到他们愤怒的声音。也许这会让你对那些付费使用你的产品的人产生同情心。有一个广为人知的故事：杰夫·贝索斯 (Jeff Bezos) 暂停会议，拨打自己的客户服务电话，并等待 10 多分钟才得到答复。上述公司的首席执行官最后一次拨打自己的客户支持热线是什么时候？体验自己的产品在工作时固然很好，但上述组织中的任何人最后一次经历“困难”的客户旅程是什么时候呢？相比之下，我最近取消了对一家小型初创公司服务的订阅。他们的高级领导团队中的某人询问是否可以打电话来谈谈我取消的原因。我说当然可以，并就他们的失败进行了半个小时的愉快的抱怨/聊天。几乎每次投诉时，他们都会回答“哦，是的，我也觉得这很烦人”，或者“嗯，我没有经历过这种情况，但我知道为什么它会很糟糕。”他们从来没有说过“我们的指标没有显示问题”或“人们真的关心这个吗？”也许我是受宠若惊了。每次会议开始时都例行公事地给呼叫中心打电话也许是浪费高层领导的时间。也许我是唯一一个当人们懒得在工作中付出最低限度的努力时感到恼火的人。但是，也许，吸入几乎没有消化的泥浆的有毒产物是让人们改善饮食的唯一方法。不可能！ ↩︎ 如果我想取消就不行。 ↩︎我宁愿把头伸进一桶不冷不热的病里！ ↩︎ 当然是用于培训和监控目的！ ↩︎

Original Summary

  1. I called a large company the other day. Did I know the information I wanted could be found on their website?0 And was I aware that I could manage my account online?1 And would I like to receive a link to chat with their AI assistant via WhatsApp?2 Naturally, call volumes were higher than expected. I can only assume that whoever was in charge of predicting call volumes had recent suffered a traumatic brain injury and was unable to count beyond five without pulling their other hand out of their fundament. The cheerful woman warbled through her pre-recorded script and was suddenly replaced with a hideous electronic monstrosity. I recorded the call3 so that you can experience this monument to synthetic glory! 🔊 💾 Download this audio file. This is from a company whose website gushes about how innovative it is. AI is transforming its business at scale! Dedicated to technological excellence and delivering ISO accredited quality in all its divisions! And yet, somewhere, someone decided that customer experience was good enough. "Dogfooding" is a sacred practice in the tech industry. Use your own products. That's it. That's all you have to do. For example, if you work for Slack - you can't use Teams for your messaging solution. You have to show people that you have faith in your own products. But it goes deeper than that. When I used to work for mobile phone networks, they asked us to spend time in call centres. It isn't enough to receive a quarterly report on customer KPIs. You have to hear the rage in customers' voices as they struggle with your billing system. Perhaps that will convince you to have empathy with the people paying to use your product. There's an oft told story about Jeff Bezos pausing a meeting to call his own customer service number - and waiting over 10 minutes for an answer. When was the last time the CEO of the above company called their own customer support line? It's all very well to experience your own product when it is working, but when was the last time anyone in the above organisation went through a "difficult" customer journey. By contrast, I recently cancelled a subscription to a small start-up's service. Someone from their senior leadership team asked if they could call to chat about why I cancelled. I said sure and had an enjoyable half-hour whinge / chat about their failings. At almost every complaint, they replied either "Oh, yeah, I also find that annoying" or "Huh, I've not experienced that, but I can see why it would suck." At no point did they ever say "Our metrics don't show a problem" or "Do people really care about that?" Maybe I was being flattered. Maybe it's a waste of senior leadership time to start every meeting with a ritual phone call to the call centre. Maybe I'm the only one who gets annoyed when people can't be bothered to put the bare minimum effort into their job. But, maybe, breathing in the noxious output of barely digested slurry is the only way to get people to improve their diet. It couldn't! ↩︎ Not if I wanted to cancel. ↩︎ I'd rather stick my head in a bucket of lukewarm sick! ↩︎ For training and monitoring purposes, of course! ↩︎

评论（默认中文）

Aurornis 2026/03/22 22:16
> 有一个广为人知的故事：杰夫·贝佐斯 (Jeff Bezos) 暂停会议，拨打自己的客户服务电话，并等待了 10 多分钟才得到答复。

我的工作之一是在一家以不健康的官僚主义和政治方式发展起来的公司。该产品对某些人来说几乎不重要，因为他们在内部玩哗众取宠的游戏，抢功劳，建立自己的帝国。

在应该讨论产品方向和优先级的会议中，我有时会拿出手机并打开应用程序，试图展示服务的一些实际问题。会议的基调会变得恐慌，因为某些产品负责人会想方设法阻止我展示真实产品的功能，而不是他们精心准备的幻灯片，为高管展示了一个非常好的故事。我因为展示实际产品而不是他们的 KPI 和图表的替代世界而成为敌人。

> There's an oft told story about Jeff Bezos pausing a meeting to call his own customer service number - and waiting over 10 minutes for an answer.

One of my jobs was at a company that had developed at unhealthy amount of bureaucracy and politics. The product barely mattered to some because they were playing internal games of grandstanding, taking credit, and building their empires.

In meetings where were supposed to be talking about product direction and priorities I would some times pull out my phone and open the app to try to demonstrate some real problem with the service. The tone of the meeting would change to panic as certain product leads would try to do anything to stop me from showing what the real product did instead of their neatly prepared slide decks that showed a much nice story for the executives. I became the enemy for showing the actual product instead of their alternate world of KPIs and charts.

在 HN 中查看
apexalpha 2026/03/22 22:34
我在一个（政府和极端官僚）组织工作，该组织构建现场工程师使用的应用程序。

我发现 SSO 被破坏了。他们必须使用同一帐户登录每个应用程序。每天两次，因为“为了安全”，代币直播时长为 4 小时。

我发现这是因为他们将这些应用程序发布为 PWA，使它们比普通应用程序更加孤立。

我问产品经理，他说问题出在“苹果和谷歌”，而不是他的部门。当被问及为什么他为应用程序选择 PWA 时，他说这更容易部署，可以节省开发人员帐户等。

由于我无法强迫他进行更改，因此我找到了一个解决方法：如果您在三星平板电脑上的最新 Android 版本上使用 Edge，则 SSO 可在 PWA 中工作。幸运的是，我们购买了三星平板电脑（这不是购买时的要求，我查了一下，只是运气）。

我向 Intune 经理询问了此事，他们说现场工程师应该将 Edge 设置为默认值，而不是 Chrome。

当在公司平板电脑上尝试此操作时，它显示：“Edge被X组策略禁用”。那个部门制定了政策...

他们删除这个后，我问为什么它不是默认浏览器，他说这是不可能的。我通过谷歌搜索 Intune 手册来设置默认浏览器，向他提出了这个问题。

后来他们表示，他们已经为此向微软提出了支持请求。

在内部 Wiki 上我找到了一个描述该问题的文档。日期是我加入前 11 个月。

I work at a (government and extreme bureaucratic) organisation that builds apps used by field engineers.

I found out SSO was broken. They had to login to every app using the same account. Twice per day because the token live was 4 hours "for security".

I found out it was because they published these apps as PWAs, making them more isolated than normal apps.

I asked the product manager and he says the issue is "with Apple and Google", not his department. When asked why he chose PWAs for the apps he said this was easier to deploy, saves them developer accounts and such.

Since I can't force him to change I found a workaround: SSO works in PWAs if you use Edge on a recent Android version on a Samsung tablet. Lucky me we had bought Samsung tablets (this was not a requirement when purchasing I looked it up, just luck).

I asked the Intune manager about this and they said the field engineers should just set Edge as default in stead of Chrome.

When trying this on a company tablet it said: "Edge disabled by X group policy". That guys' department set the policy...

After they removed this I asked why it wasn't the default browser and he said this wasn't possible. I challenged him on this by Googling the Intune manual to set the default browser.

Later they said they had raised a support ticket with Microsoft for this.

On the internal Wiki I found a document describing the problem. It was dated 11 months before I joined.

arbirk 2026/03/23 00:26
我曾经在一家政府机构工作，那里有 4 名员工使用在 IE6 上运行的应用程序。所以剩下的2000强组织不得不使用chrome通过远程桌面变成服务器。

决策树：还有部门仍在使用 IE6 吗？是 -> 让我们设置一个远程桌面集群，以便其他人可以使用 Chrome

I once worked in a government agency where 4 employees used a app that on ran on IE6. So the rest of the 2000 strong organization had to use chrome by remote desktop into a server.

Decision tree: Does any department still use IE6? Yes -> lets setup a Remote Desktop cluster so the rest can use Chrome

在 HN 中查看
antisthenes 2026/03/22 22:59
简而言之：除了自己的薪水和下午 5 点下班，没有人关心任何事情。

这是人类的状况（部分也是公司自己的错，因为他们停止了对员工的投资）

那些在意并充满热情的人最终会加入到其他 99.9% 的行列，因为拉着 10 个不在乎的贪图便宜的人的车绝对是令人筋疲力尽的。

我羡慕那些能在任何一份工作上工作超过 2-3 年的人。我想，成为自己的老板是在足够长的时间内保持热情和关心某件事的少数方法之一。

The short of it is: no one gives a shit about anything but their own paycheck and getting off of work at 5pm.

It's the human condition (and also in part the companies' own fault since they stopped investing in employees)

The people who give a shit and are passionate eventually join the other 99.9%, because it's absolutely exhausting pulling the cart with 10 freeloaders on it who don't care.

I envy the people who can give a shit for longer than 2-3 years at any given job. I suppose being your own boss is one of the few ways to stay passionate and care about something for a long enough period of time.

apexalpha 2026/03/22 23:00
我愿意！

我把这些人塞进一个房间，现在问题已经解决了。

该系统仍然很糟糕，但 2000 名现场工程师又恢复了 10 分钟的工作时间。

几周后，PWA 团队的 Scrum Master 在一次会议上就此发表了鼓舞人心的演讲。

就我个人而言，你不能因为我的应用程序这么长时间以来一直如此糟糕而折磨我，但是是的。

I do!

I border collied these people into a room and the issue is now fixed.

The system still sucks but 2000 field engineers got 10 min of their days back.

A few weeks later the Scrum Master of the PWA team gave an inspiring talk about it at a conference.

Personally you couldn't torture out of me that my app was so bad for so long, but yeah.

antisthenes 2026/03/22 23:34
有趣的是……我碰巧有一只边境牧羊犬，它们仅仅通过观察人类就能按照自己的方式完成任务的能力是……不可思议的。

我将不得不考虑这样的事情，可以在整个程序范围内节省 5-10 分钟的时间。

Interesting...I happen to have a border collie and their ability to get things done their way simply by looking at humans is...uncanny.

I will have to think of things like this where can save 5-10 min on something program-wide.

dxdm 2026/03/22 23:28
我看到一个有趣的比喻，指的是安排会议、让人们加入并找到解决方案等繁琐的工作。以这种方式阅读它让我的一天变得更加明亮。我也需要那个。

顺便说一句，边境牧羊犬是很棒的狗，绵羊也很棒。我发现用它们来代替我们人类的弱点并不是自动的不尊重。意图很重要。

GP，请不要灰心。

I see a fun metaphor for doing the tedious work of arranging a meeting, getting people to join, and getting a solution. Reading it put this way made my day a little brighter. I needed that, too.

Btw, border collies are awesome dogs, and sheep are also awesome. I find no automatic disrespect in using them as stand-ins for our human foibles; intent matters.

GP, please don't be discouraged.

在 HN 中查看
tonyarkles 2026/03/22 23:55
我第一次真正接触边境牧羊犬是在一次家庭聚会上。公园里有一群孩子跑来跑去玩耍。有一次，有人带着一只边境牧羊犬出现，我高兴又惊讶地看着这只狗做着放牧的事情，慢慢地、小心地把这群孩子拉得更近。孩子们甚至没有意识到这一点，直到他们彼此靠得太近，无法轻松地玩捉人游戏。主人把狗叫了回来，比赛继续进行。

后来我养了一只牧羊犬，它有很强的放牧本能。尽管与其他狗和猫在一起，但她基本上只是表现得像有趣的警察。可爱的生物！

放羊也是一种很有趣的经历。我能描述的最好的方式是每只羊周围都有一个非常大的肥皂泡。您需要轻轻地推动气泡，让它们也到达您想要的位置。如果你用力过猛，泡沫就会破裂，它们就会散开，你就必须后退一步，让泡沫重新形成。

One of my first real experiences with Border Collies was at a family reunion. There were a bunch of kids running around playing in the park. At one point someone showed up with a border collie and I watched with delight and amazement as the dog did the herding thing and slowly and carefully pushed the group of children closer together. The kids didn't even realize it until they were way too close to each other to comfortably play tag. The owner called the dog back and the games continued.

Later on I ended up with a sheltie with a very strong herding instinct. She mostly just acted like the Fun Police though with the other dog and cats. Lovely creatures!

Herding sheep is such an interesting experience too. The best way I can describe it is that each sheep has a really large soap bubble around them. You need to push gently on the bubble to get them to go where you want them too. If you push too hard and the bubble pops, they'll scatter and you have to step back and let the bubble reform.

losvedir 2026/03/22 23:18
不太喜欢隐喻？我个人很欣赏他们描述的将所有相关人类聚集在一起的方式（圈养？牧羊？放牧？许多常见的英语畜牧业表达）。
Not much a fan of metaphor? I personally appreciated the way they described about getting (corralling? shepherding? herding? Lots of common animal husbandry expressions in English) all the relevant humans together.
在 HN 中查看
primitivesuave 2026/03/22 23:15
英语中充满了以动物为基础的隐喻，这是一个相当无伤大雅的说法。 “我把每个人都赶到一个房间里”并不自动意味着人们将这些人视为动物。
English is full of animal-based metaphors, and that's a pretty innocuous statement. "I herded everyone into a room" does not automatically imply that one perceives those people as animals.

testaccount28 2026/03/22 23:19
在这个比喻中，OP就是狗。
in this analogy, it is OP who is the dog.
在 HN 中查看
jrockway 2026/03/22 23:46
我认为品种的选择是有意义的。 边境牧羊犬是最聪明的狗品种，它的起源是放牧羊。 称你的同事为绵羊并不是特别好。 称自己为最聪明的狗品种并不是特别谦虚。 这就是您回复的人反对的原因。
I think the choice of breed has meaning. The border collie is the smartest breed of dog, and its origin is in herding sheep. Calling your coworkers sheep isn't particularly nice. Calling yourself the smartest breed of dog isn't particularly humble. That's why the person you're replying to objects.
在 HN 中查看
phil21 2026/03/23 00:23
荒谬的。我们应该谴责那些被冒犯的人。它减少了应该考虑的真正冒犯情况的影响和严重性。

在我的整个职业生涯中，我都曾放牧过猫、分类过羊、驯过牛。我可以想出更多更符合实际情况的建议。

我比在另一边更可能扮演猫、羊和牛。

这只是与人类群体合作的一部分。我们在群体中会变得愚蠢，从而导致群体行为。通常需要有人照顾我们才能改掉这个习惯。

Ridiculous. We should be calling people out for being performatively offended. It reduces the impact and gravitas of situations where real offense is given that should be considered.

I have herded cats, sorted sheep, and wrangled cattle all throughout my career. I can come up with more that are quite accurate to the situation.

And I've been the cat, sheep, and cattle likely more than I have been on the other side.

It's simply part of working with groups of humans. We become dumb in groups and lend ourselves towards herd behavior. It often requires someone tending to us to break us of the habit.

dpark 2026/03/23 00:11
这是一个非常折磨人的逻辑，要找到一些值得冒犯的事情。

> 称自己为最聪明的狗品种并不特别谦虚

当然，“像边境牧羊犬一样聪明”对于人类来说并不是一个很高的标准。

This is some really tortured logic to find something to get offended about.

> Calling yourself the smartest breed of dog isn't particularly humble

Surely “smart as a border collie” is not a high bar for a human.

在 HN 中查看
adiabatichottub 2026/03/23 00:03
这可能是对这个比喻的解读太多了。我认为这是恰当的，因为不管别人聪明不聪明，我们都有盲点，为了把需要做的事情做好，你必须以正确的方式施加压力，克服一定的群体惯性。 这些事情仍然符合这个比喻，但不一定是不尊重。
That's probably reading too much into the metaphor. I think it's apropos because regardless of whether the others are smart or not, we all have blind sides, and in order to get things done that need to be done you have to apply pressure in the right way to overcome a certain amount of group inertia. Those things still fit with the metaphor without necessarily being disrespectful.

I have a relative that has a border collie and the thing has got to have the dog version of Down syndrome.

testaccount28 2026/03/23 00:28

边境牧羊犬也可以咬人类的脚后跟。

在 HN 中查看
nocman 2026/03/22 23:31
使用的“图片”一词绝对没有任何问题。 我建议不要对别人的话（尤其是在网上）做出最坏的解释。 我们大多数人可能至少有时会这样做（包括现在的公司），但最好让人们从怀疑中受益。 在这种情况下，我认为可以公平地假设，最初的发帖人只是说他不会让他们试图逃跑，而没有真正解决问题——就像边境牧羊犬阻止其他动物偏离群体，将它们留在需要的地方一样。

没有必要假设他们的意思是会议中的其他人不那么重要或不那么聪明，或者其他什么。 也许他们只是对解决这个问题不太感兴趣。

There's absolutely nothing wrong with the word picture that was used. I'd advise against assuming the worst possible interpretation of someone else's words (especially online). Most of us probably do that at least sometimes (present company included), but it would be much better to give people the benefit of the doubt. In this case, I think it is fair to assume that the original poster was just saying that he wouldn't let them try to get away and not actually deal with the problem -- much as a border collie prevents other animals from straying from the group, keeping them where they need to be.

There is no need to assume that they meant that the others in the meeting were less important or less intelligent, or whatever. They were, perhaps, just less interested in dealing with the problem.

我只是 他妈的羞耻 我们整个 IT 部门的人员都参与其中，因此我决定解决这个问题。

我想，这是总理第一次与现场工程师交谈。

'corralled' better for you?

And yeah I did that. It wasn't even my app. Or my team. Or my field engineers.

I was just fucking ashamed of our entire IT department and thus took it upon me to fix this.

It was the first time the PM had ever spoken to a field engineer, I think.

CalChris 2026/03/23 00:36

TonyStr 2026/03/23 19:30
您可以向您的经理反映，您每天为 2000 名工人节省了 10 分钟的时间，相当于每年 8333 个工作小时，相当于每年 200 万美元，并认为您带来的这一价值应该反映在您的工资中。您还可以将其添加到您的简历中，以使您对其他雇主更具吸引力。最终，如果你想要更高的薪水，你就必须努力让别人相信你的价值。
You can take it up with your manager that you saved 10 minutes of time per day for 2000 workers, equivalent to 8333 work hours per year, equivalent to $2M USD per year, and argue that this value you bring should be reflected in your salary. You can also add it to your CV, to make you more attractive to other employers. Ultimately, you will have to make an effort to convince others of your value if you want a higher salary.
在 HN 中查看
deaux 2026/03/23 00:25
不管你信不信，有些地方 50% 或更多的人对此很在意。

我确信在乔布斯领导下的苹果公司，这一比例会非常高。 现在它已经大幅下降了。

不过，在国家范围内，你是绝对正确的。

Believe it or not, there exist places where 50% or more give a shit.

I'm sure at Apple under Jobs, the % would've been very high. It will have dropped significantly by now.

You're absolutely right on a country-scale though.

在 HN 中查看
wolvoleo 2026/03/23 14:20
> 那些在意并充满热情的人最终会加入其他 99.9% 的行列，因为拉着 10 个不在乎的贪图便宜的人的车绝对是令人筋疲力尽的。

这不适合我。人们了解到该公司是一堆空话，所有的使命宣言和企业价值观都是胡说八道，只有底线才重要。剩下的只是为了鼓舞士气。领导什么都不管，只要他们方便，就会把我们踢到路边。

一旦你看透了那些废话，所有的激情就会消失，你就会像其他人一样尽可能少地做事。

好吧，我们中的一些人如果是一架优秀的小无人机，可能会得到晋升，但我不是管理材料，所以我只是找一个安静的角落，在那里我可以用最少的工作量领取工资。假装所有的热情。只需让人工智能在全体员工会议时发布一些带有表情符号的兴奋评论即可。

无论如何，这就是让我不再关心的原因。我也曾经很关心。有时我仍然这样做，但这是因为我有一些项目，我在个人/技术层面上确实觉得有趣。但大部分工作都是消除其他团队为我们弥补的废话繁文缛节。我现在只关心我的个人发展。

> The people who give a shit and are passionate eventually join the other 99.9%, because it's absolutely exhausting pulling the cart with 10 freeloaders on it who don't care.

It's not that for me. It's learning that the company is a bunch of hot air, all the mission statements and corporate values are total bullshit and only the bottom line matters. The rest is just pr for morale. The leadership doesn't care about anything and will kick us to the kerb whenever it's convenient for them.

Once you look through that bullshit all passion disappears and you just do as little as possible like all the others.

Well, some of us might get promoted if they are a good little drone but I'm not management material so I'm just finding a quiet corner where I can collect my salary with the least amount of work. And just fake all the enthusiasm. Just get AI to post some emoji loaded excited comments when there's an all hands teams meeting.

Anyway that's what turned me off giving a fuck. I did use to care too. Sometimes I still do but it's because I have some project I actually find interesting on a personal/technical level. But most of the work is cutting through bullshit red tape that other teams have made up for us. I'm just caring about my personal development now.

sgarland 2026/03/22 22:49
我确实不明白公司是如何走到这一步的，尤其是当最高管理层都是创始人时（或者也许情况更糟？）。我可以理解人们如何想让他们的老板高兴，这可能会导致不断的胡说八道，但在某些时候，为什么首席技术官/首席执行官等不说“我要去与员工交谈以了解他们的观点？”

尽管美国核海军存在许多缺陷，但它还是正确地做到了这一点。一般来说，海军反应堆负责人——四星上将——每年至少一次巡视每艘舰艇，其中可能包括短暂的航行期。在这次访问期间，海军上将将与机舱值班人员交谈，所有高级领导层均被撤职。他们会问日常生活怎么样，他们觉得什么有挑战性或烦人，他们喜欢什么，等等。显然，有很多自我过滤的情况发生（尽管有时不是——海军核武器不以社交优雅而闻名），而且初级值班员觉得烦人的事情可能只是工作的必需部分，但收集了一些有用的信号。

即使在核计划之外，朱姆沃尔特上将也是一个突出的例子，他在担任海军作战部长期间实施了 70 项不同的变革，这些变革的直接结果是与水手交谈，所有这些变革的目的都是为了提高生活质量、效率或沟通。

I legitimately don’t understand how companies get to this point, especially when the C-suite is full of founders (or maybe that’s worse?). I can understand how people want to make their bosses happy, and that can cascade into constant bullshitting, but at some point why doesn’t the CTO / CEO / etc. say “I’m going to go have conversations with the workers to get their perspective?”

The U.S. Nuclear Navy, for all of its many flaws, gets this right. Generally at least once a year, the head of Naval Reactors - a four-star Admiral - tours every vessel, which may include a brief underway period. During this tour, the Admiral will talk to the engine room watchstanders, with all senior leadership removed. They’ll ask how daily life is, what they find challenging or annoying, what they like, etc. There’s obviously a lot of self-filtering (though sometimes not - Navy Nukes are not known for their social graces) that occurs, and also what a junior watchstander finds annoying may just be a required part of the job, but some useful signal is gathered.

Even outside of the nuclear program, one standout example was Admiral Zumwalt, who as Chief of Naval Operations implemented 70 different changes over his tenure as a direct result of talking to sailors, all of which were designed to improve quality of life, efficiency, or communication.

在 HN 中查看
arjie 2026/03/23 00:16
事实是你无法让任何人做你想做的事。你可以尝试让他们做你想做的事，但最终他们却是别人。沃尔特·罗利爵士挚爱的朋友兼副官是劳伦斯·凯米斯。当罗利被赦免并被送回寻找埃尔多拉多时，设定的条件是他不应该攻击西班牙人。他前往南美，派出一支由他的密友和知己率领的分遣队上岸——他是一个你能想象到的最团结的人。凯米斯违反明确命令袭击了西班牙前哨基地，杀死了罗利的儿子，回到罗利将此事告知他，然后立即自杀。然而，这一切都不会帮助罗利，因为现在他的赦免条件被违反了，他按照承诺被处决了。
The truth is you can't get anyone to do what you want. You can try to make them do what you want but they're someone else in the end. Sir Walter Raleigh's beloved friend and lieutenant was Lawrence Kemys. When Raleigh was pardoned and sent back to find El Dorado, the conditions set were that he should not attack the Spaniards. He goes out to South America, and puts a detachment ashore led by his close friend and confidant - as aligned a person as you can imagine. Kemys attacks a Spanish outpost against express orders, gets Raleigh's son killed, returns to Raleigh to inform him of this, and promptly commits suicide. None of this is going to help Raleigh, though, because the conditions of his pardon now having been violated he is executed as promised.
在 HN 中查看
cbg0 2026/03/22 22:57
> 在某些时候，为什么首席技术官/首席执行官等不说“我要去与员工交谈以了解他们的观点？”

当您从经理那里得到的所有报告都是 5/5 星并且“一切都很棒”时，您为什么还要这么做呢？一旦一个组织变得足够大，到达最高管理层的信息就会经过层层过滤，以至于它几乎不再像现实了，即使你从等式中消除了恶意。

> at some point why doesn’t the CTO / CEO / etc. say “I’m going to go have conversations with the workers to get their perspective?”

Why would you when all of the reports you're getting from your managers are 5/5 stars and "everything's great". Once an organization gets large enough, the information that reaches C-suite has been filtered through so many layers that it barely resembles reality anymore, even when you remove malice from the equation.

在 HN 中查看
apexalpha 2026/03/22 22:58
在我的组织里，首席信息官他妈的对计算机了如指掌。就个人而言，他是个好人，但即使他 3 个孩子的生命依赖于 Vim，他也无法戒掉 Vim。

他被安排在那里是因为他以前在公司工作了很多年，而且他领导其他部门的表现很好。

由于他无法评估任何与 IT 相关的事情，他只能依赖下属的“建议”，这些人试图通过夸大部门的重要性来为其部门获得最多的预算。

他下面的这一层主要是产品经理、RTE 等...（我们是安全敏捷！开发人员速度，哇哦！）。

他们对计算机也不太了解，即使了解，也是非常特定领域的，例如 SAP 等。

这些人试图通过夸大其重要性来争取预算。他们通过拥有更多的应用程序和更多的人依赖它们来证明这一点。

“看看我们处理了 2000 张支持请求，如果没有我们，公司就会陷入瘫痪！”。

别介意有 2000 张支持票 是一件坏事。而且主要是由他们的糟糕应用程序造成的。

这种情况一直持续下去。我有10年的工程师经验，想看看“另一面”，但太累了。

几个月前，一位“隐私官”询问为什么我们员工的名字和姓氏会出现在 Active Directory 中，并命令他们降低隐私风险。

他们没有具体说明有哪些风险。即使被问到也无法清楚地表达出来。他们也没有透露何时风险会“足够”降低。

该团队感到恐慌，因为他们现在“不遵守”公司政策。

我必须亲自干预，以确保这一单一指令不会使我们整个公司脱轨。

At my org the CIO knows fuck all about computers. Great guy on a personal level but wouldn't be able to quit Vim even if the lives of his 3 kids depended on it.

He was put there because he was with the company for years before and he led other departments fine.

Since he can't evaluate anything IT related himself he relies on 'advice' from the people beneath him who try to get the most budget for their departments by overstating how important they are.

This layer beneath him is mostly product managers, RTEs etc... (We are SAFE Agile! Developer Velocity, Woohoo!).

They also don't know much about computer and if they do it's very domain specific, such as SAP or so.

These people try to fight for budget by overstating their importance. They demonstrate this by having more apps and more people relying on them.

"Look we handled 2000 support tickets, the company would grind to a halt without us!".

Never mind that having 2000 support tickets is a bad thing. And also mostly caused by their shitty apps.

This keeps going on and on. I have 10 years experience as engineer and wanted to see "the other side" but it's so exhausting.

A few months back a 'privacy officer' asked why the first and last names of our employees were in the Active Directory and ordered them to reduce the privacy risks.

They failed to specify what risks. Couldn't articulate them even when asked. They also didn't say when the risk would've been reduced 'enough'.

The team was panicking as they were now 'non-compliant' with company policy.

I had to intervene personally to make sure this single directive didn't derail our entire company.

在 HN 中查看
sgarland 2026/03/22 23:05
> 不要介意拥有 2000 张支持票是一件坏事。而且主要是由他们的糟糕应用程序造成的。

我经常不得不与人们抗争，不要添加新的、不可操作的警报，作为对事件的下意识反应。我发誓思维过程是“发生了事件，我们添加了新的警报 - 看，我们很主动！”而不是解决根本原因。

> Never mind that having 2000 support tickets is a bad thing. And also mostly caused by their shitty apps.

I’m constantly having to fight people to not add new, inactionable alerts as knee-jerk reactions to incidents. I swear the thought process is “an incident happened, we added a new alert - look, we’re proactive!” instead of, you know, fixing the root causes.

在 HN 中查看
apexalpha 2026/03/22 23:14
去年夏天，当 WeTransfer 突然改变人工智能培训政策时，整个 CISO 部门都惊慌失措，整个网站被封锁。

从流量中我们可以看到，公司中有 12% 的人每天使用该网站，在我们的工程师和承包商之间传输千兆字节的数据。

我问为什么我们不开始向 WeTransfer 付费，因为它使用如此广泛，而且这也可以解决问题。

他们说他们应该只使用内部 SharePoint 文件共享工具。

我问这将如何运作，因为 WeTransfer 的大部分用途是我们接收文档，而不是共享它们。

他们说承包商应该更新他们的政策，辩论就结束了。

上次我与一位现场工程师交谈时，他说他们现在主要使用私人邮箱，因为他们“甚至无法再在公司笔记本电脑上复制 Outlook 中的内容”。

我决定不向 CISO 报告此事，这些文档是工单和证明工单已完成的图片。一天后它们就变得无关紧要了。

When WeTransfer suddenty changed it's policy for AI training last summer the entire CISO department panicked and had the entire website blocked.

In traffic we could see that 12% of the company used the site daily, transferring gigabytes of data between our engineers and contractors.

I asked why we didn't just start paying WeTransfer since it's so widely used and this would solve the problem, too.

They said they should just use the internal SharePoint file sharing tool.

I asked how this would work since most of WeTransfer use was us receiving docs, not sharing them.

They said the contractors should just update their policy, and that was the end of the debate.

Last time I spoke to a field engineer he said they mostly use private mailboxes now mostly since they "can't even copy something in Outlook anymore" on company laptops.

I decided not to report this to CISO and these docs are workorders and pictures demonstrating workorders have been completed. They're irrelevant one day later.

在 HN 中查看
rcxdude 2026/03/23 11:53
> 询问这将如何工作，因为 WeTransfer 的大部分用途是我们接收文档，而不是共享它们。

您也可以在共享点中执行此操作。这不是最好的，但您可以创建一个仅用于上传到文件夹的链接，并将其发送给某人，以便他们可以向您发送文件，而无需自己创建帐户。

...如果它没有被安全策略禁用，就像微软产品中几乎所有其他模糊有用的功能似乎被 IT 组织禁用一样。

> asked how this would work since most of WeTransfer use was us receiving docs, not sharing them.

You can do this in sharepoint as well. It's not the best, but you can make a link which is for upload-only to a folder and send it to someone so they can send you files without needing an account themselves.

...if it's not disabled by security policy like almost every other vague useful feature in a microsoft product seems to be by IT organisations.

在 HN 中查看
SenHeng 2026/03/23 13:45
我曾经和同事吃寿司后食物中毒。他们非常认真地想就如何防止这种情况再次发生进行辩论。什么？我是不是应该再也不吃寿司了？

在重要演讲之前避免食用可能挥发性的食物？可以理解。

只要您在这个团队工作，就避免吃寿司？滚蛋吧。

I once had food poisoning after having sushi with colleagues. They very seriously wanted to have a debate about how we can prevent it from happening again. What? Am I suppose to never eat sushi ever again?

Avoid possibly volatile foods before an important presentation? Understandable.

Avoid sushi for as long as you're employed in this team? Fuck off.

budman1 2026/03/23 00:17
退出 vi 是一项基本能力测试。

你会听一个不会缝合伤口的医生的话吗？ 机械师无法从棘轮上拆下套筒怎么办？

简单的文件编辑。 vi 已经存在于每个。 如果您还没有看到过它，并且至少需要过一次，那么您一直在做什么？

（个人轶事：曾经有一位工程副总裁提出，文档中的流浪“：wq”是真正工程师的标志......在他应该在的地方之外工作......）

quitting vi is a basic competency test.

would you listen to a doctor that could not suture a cut? how about a mechanic that could not remove a socket from a ratchet?

simple file editing. vi has been around for every. if you haven't seen it, and needed it at least once, what have you been doing?

(personal anecdote: once had an engineering VP bring up that a stray ":wq" in a document was a sign of a real engineer...working outside of where he should be..)

在 HN 中查看
vovavili 2026/03/23 01:08
这更像是一个廉价的陷阱，而不是一个有效的测试。如果我们像我这样的人，我学会了在 IDEA/PyCharm 中编码，这些天主要使用 Zed 或 OpenCode 进行编码，偶尔也会使用 nano 和 Positron。如果不查找它，我就无法在 Neovim 中做任何事情，因为我没有理由学习它。一位在 20 世纪学习过适当实践的医生现在可能会因为了解这些实践知识而被聘用。
It's more of a cheap gotcha than a valid test. If we take somebody like me, I learned to code in IDEA/PyCharm, these days mostly code with either Zed or OpenCode, and occasionally drop into nano and Positron. I wouldn't be able to do anything in Neovim without looking it up simply because I had no reason to learn it. A doctor who learned practices appropriate in the 20th century might now necessarily be hired for knowledge of these practices today.
在 HN 中查看
Quarrelsome 2026/03/23 00:24
> 退出 vi 是一项基本能力测试。

不，这是流行测验的废话。哦，你知道“:wq”吗？干得好！但如果你不知道，你多做几次，现在你就知道了。除了教你一些关于糟糕的用户体验的知识之外，它什么也没做。

> 如果您还没有看到过它，并且至少需要过一次，那么您一直在做什么？

使用其他可用的 ide 之一？

> 曾经有一位工程副总裁提出，文档中的“:wq”是真正工程师的标志......在他应该在的地方之外工作......

这并不是良好判断力的标志，而是技术时尚的标志。这是时髦的狗屎，类似于拒绝候选人，因为他们是泰勒·斯威夫特的粉丝，却不知道 Tool 乐队是谁。

> quitting vi is a basic competency test.

no, its pop quiz bullshit. Oh you know about ":wq"? Well done! But if you don't know, you do it a few times and now you know. It does nothing, outside of teaching you a bit about poor UX.

> if you haven't seen it, and needed it at least once, what have you been doing?

using one of the other available ides?

> once had an engineering VP bring up that a stray ":wq" in a document was a sign of a real engineer...working outside of where he should be..

That's not a sign of good judgement, that's a sign of being technically fashionable. It's hipster shit, akin to rejecting a candidate because they're a fan of Taylor Swift and don't know who the band Tool are.

cromka 2026/03/23 16:20

johnisgood 2026/03/23 01:49
了解 vim 与了解几乎任何其他东西有什么区别？如果您申请科技行业的工作，您应该知道，长按电源按钮，您的电脑就会关闭。这也是流行测验吗？显然，现在的门槛低得离谱。
What distinguishes knowing about vim from knowing about virtually anything else? If you apply to a job in tech, you should know that by long-pressing the power button, your PC turns off. Is this pop quiz shit, too? The bar is ridiculously low these days, apparently.
在 HN 中查看
Quarrelsome 2026/03/23 02:30
你是在讽刺吗？它与电源按钮完全不同。每个人都必须按电源按钮，但不是每个人都必须使用 Vi。

:wq 是退出应用程序的最疯狂的组合键之一，这只是时髦的狗屎，使用 vim 的人认为他们是唯一的“真正的工程师”。这只是一种令人厌恶的傲慢和自慰。重要的是代码，而不是 IDE。将其作为技术卓越的标志来关注它，是对工程师应该关心的事情（计算机科学的事情）的嘲弄，并以高中恶霸贬低其他不穿耐克鞋的孩子的所有优雅来取而代之。

are you being sarcastic? Its nothing like the power button. Everyone has to press the power button but not everyone has to use Vi.

:wq is one of the most insane key combinations to quit an app and this is just hipster shit where people who use vim think they're the only "real engineers". It's just a disgusting level of arrogance and masturbation. The code is what matters, not the IDE. To focus on it as a sign of technical excellence makes a mockery of what engineers are supposed to care about (comp sci things) and replaces them with all the elegance of a high school bully belittling some other kid for not wearing Nikes.

在 HN 中查看
pavlus 2026/03/23 06:21
这不是关于使用 vim，而是关于当你必须在第一次访问的服务器上 sudoedit 一个配置时，它有 vim 作为默认编辑器，所以你必须知道如何退出它并打开 nano，或任何你使用的东西。这是关于接触，接触很多小事情是经验的标志，准确地说是经验。如果他们不知道一些基本的小事情，那就表明他们没有相关经验。还可以检查他们的文件末尾是否有空行，知道如何从机器中删除 docker 映像，或者得到一个 TCP/UDP 笑话。
It's not about using vim, it's about when you had to sudoedit a config on a server you visited the first time, and it had vim as default EDITOR, so you have to know how to exit it and open nano, or whatever you use. It's about exposure, exposure to many small things is a sign of experience, it is experience, to be precise. If they don't know small basic things, it's a sign, they don't have relevant experience. Can also check, if they have empty lines at the end of their files, know how to remove docker images from their machine, or get a TCP/UDP joke.
在 HN 中查看
Quarrelsome 2026/03/23 06:37
这是关于想要雇用自己的人。这是我们所有人都遭受的认知偏见。完全没有意识到这一点，并纯粹根据自己的经验来测试别人，就是：准确地说，缺乏经验，意识到其他人可以和你一样优秀，但却走了完全不同的道路。

不，我敢打赌他们很糟糕，为什么他们不能做我已经做过的事情呢？

Its about people wanting to hire themselves. It's a cognitive bias we all suffer from. Being completely unaware of it and purity testing others based on your own experience, is: to be precise, a lack of experience in realising that other people can be just as good as you but took a completely different path to get there.

Nah, I bet they suck, why can't they do what I've already done?

在 HN 中查看
pavlus 2026/03/23 08:09
是的，这就是我们正在寻找的偏见。如果你无法退出 vim，不知道前端如何与后端通信，无法在不看字母的情况下在常规 qwerty 键盘上打字，或者不使用鼠标导航 UI，从未使用过调试器，不知道如何检查它是否为 DNS，在与利益相关者交谈后无法编写功能规范（并代表他们捍卫其优先级），或者不知道如何在浏览器中打开开发工具 - 你还没有准备好羊群猫呢，他们会把你赶下悬崖。没关系，总有一天你会到达那里。
Yes, this is the bias we are looking for. If you can't quit vim, don't know how your frontend communicates with your backend, can't type on a regular qwerty keyboard without looking at letters, or navigate UI without mouse, never used a debugger, don't know how to check if it was DNS, can't write a spec for a feature, after talking to stakeholders (and defend it's priority on their behalf), or don't know how to open dev tools in your browser - you are not ready to herd the cats yet, they will herd you off the cliff instead. And that's okay, you'll get there someday.

（FWIW我同意帕夫卢斯的观点。）

在 HN 中查看
Quarrelsome 2026/03/23 16:06
我仍然认为你只是想雇佣自己，这有点恶心。这就像身为英国人而拒绝雇用不读过或不了解 theregister.co.uk 的人一样。当然，大多数人可能应该这样做，但可能有一些伟大的开发人员不这样做，这对“其他”他们来说是极其残酷的。

vim 总是很高兴地告诉你它正在运行吗？它曾经是我使用过的设备上的默认比较工具，IIRC 在某些情况下它只是一个空白的外壳，所以在不知情的情况下找出它是否在其中可能并不简单。

所以，是的，作为一个挑战，它可能很有趣，但除了“你喜欢 vim”之外，它并不能证明是狗屎。我喜欢 Jetbrains 产品，但我不会根据某人如何使用这些产品来评估某人的能力，而是根据他们使用这些产品所创造的内容来评估某人的能力。

I still think you're just trying to hire yourself and its kinda gross. Its like being British and refusing to hire someone that doesn't read or know about theregister.co.uk. Sure, most people probably should but there might be a great dev out there who don't and its extremely cruel to "other" them.

Does vim always happily tell you that its running? It used to be the default diff tool on devices I worked with and IIRC it was just a blank shell in some cases, so it might be non-trivial to work out if you're in it, without knowing it.

So yea, its probably interesting as a challenge but it doesn't prove shit, outside of "you like vim". I like Jetbrains products but I wouldn't assess someone's ability based on how someone might use the products, but rather what they create by using them.

johnisgood 2026/03/23 16:13

编辑：您一直在大量编辑您的评论，所以我将保持原样。仅供参考，您是唯一一个谈论看门的人。

Since you are denying the importance of baseline exposure (consequently accumulated experience), I will just say this: if you are unable to figure out how to quit vim, it absolutely proves quite a lot about your experience. johnisgood 2026/03/23 17:26

Are you telling me that when you got vim opened, you panicked and could not figure it out in a relatively short timeframe?

在 HN 中查看
Quarrelsome 2026/03/23 17:56
当 vim auto 打开时，我旁边的家伙进行归纳，告诉我我需要输入“:wq”，然后开玩笑说它是多么不直观。

> FWIW 我仍然坚信，这通常是衡量您是否有使用不熟悉的工具的经验的一个不错的指标

如果你一开始就这么说我会同意，但那是不同的。优秀的工程师是勇敢的，能够潜入一些未知的领域来完成工作。我确信有很多次我们都陷入了必须使用不熟悉的技术来解决问题或抛出一些基础设施的情况。然而，就我个人而言，我从来没有使用过 vi，所以它没有出现，但这可能是因为我的背景主要是 Windows 背景，而不是 linux/unix 背景。

When vim auto opened the bloke next to me doing my induction told me I needed to type ":wq" and then made a joke about how unintuitive it is.

> FWIW I still maintain the belief that it is usually a decent indicator of whether you have got the experience to work through unfamiliar tools

if you just said that in the first place I'd agree, but that's different. Good engineers are brave and able to just dive into some unknown to get the job done. I'm sure there's been plenty of times where we've been thrust into scenarios where we have to use unfamiliar tech to solve an issue or throw up some infra. However, personally I've never _had_ to use vi, so its not come up, but that's probably because my background is very much a windows one, not a linux/unix one.

在 HN 中查看
johnisgood 2026/03/23 18:15
很公平。

> 优秀的工程师是勇敢的，能够潜入未知领域来完成工作。

我同意。我们（我）一直这样做。

回到“流行测验”这一点：我们是否同意提出松散相关的琐事问题并不是评估能力的最有效方法？我认为给予一项任务和解决它的空间更有意义。老实说，我不知道如何立即实现堆排序，但我知道如何查找其伪代码及其工作原理，然后从那里我可以将其转换为工作代码。

> 这可能是因为我的背景主要是 Windows 背景，而不是 linux/unix 背景。

是的，这就解释了。我觉得这根本没有问题。重要的是在需要时弥合差距的能力。

Fair enough.

> Good engineers are brave and able to just dive into some unknown to get the job done.

I agree. We (I) do it all the time.

Just to circle back on the "pop quiz" point: do we agree that asking loosely relevant trivia questions is not the most effective way to assess ability? I would argue it is more meaningful to be given a task and the space to solve it. To be honest, I do not know how to implement heapsort off the top of my head, but I know how to look up its pseudocode and how it works, and from there, I can translate that into working code.

> that's probably because my background is very much a windows one, not a linux/unix one.

Yes, that explains it. I find no issue with that at all. What matters is the ability to bridge gaps when needed.

在 HN 中查看
Quarrelsome 2026/03/23 19:04
> 我们是否同意提出松散相关的琐事问题并不是评估能力的最有效方法？

是的。我最近一直在面试，最好的面试是那些最像工作的面试，你要么去现场并与一些玩具示例配对，要么得到一些糟糕的现有代码，并得到一定的时间来实现该代码中的特定目标。

此类练习完成后产生的对话可能比练习本身更有价值。

> do we agree that asking loosely relevant trivia questions is not the most effective way to assess ability?

yeah. I been interviewing recently and the best interviews are those most like the job, where you either go in on-site and pair with some toy example, or get given some awful existing code and be given an amount of time to achieve a particular goal within that code.

The conversations that fall out of such exercises after they're completed are probably more valuable than the exercise itself.

在 HN 中查看
SilasX 2026/03/22 23:57
关于此的有趣/信息丰富的故事：有一个项目，OpenHatch.org c。 2011 年，试图让人们熟悉编程，为开源项目做出贡献。在其中一篇教程中（我想介绍命令行 git？），如果你按照说明进行操作，它会将你转入 vim。那时它还没有向你介绍 vim 或解释发生了什么，所以你甚至不知道如何用 google 搜索错误。这是一个应该主要关注新手友好的项目！
Funny/informative story about this: There was a project, OpenHatch.org c. 2011 that tried to get people to be comfortable enough with programming to contribute to open source projects. In one of the tutorials (I think introducing command-line git?), if you followed the instructions, it would dump you into vim. It hadn't introduced to you vim by that point or explained that's what was happening, so you wouldn't even know enough to google the error. And this was a project that was supposed to be primarily focused on being newbie-friendly!
在 HN 中查看
edent 2026/03/23 00:30
> 我确实不明白公司是如何走到这一步的

创办公司的人关心 深 关于他们试图解决的问题。

前 N 名员工也很关心——他们愿意冒险为初创公司工作，因为他们看到了潜力并希望提供帮助。

但随后你雇佣了一名会计师。会计师不关心你的使命。你只是他们的另一个客户。你需要有人接电话、扫地、设计标志等等。他们为什么要关心？你没有付钱给他们关心，他们没有投资于公司或公司的成功，他们相信他们为公司所做的任何事情都不会改变他们的个人命运。

然后，在你意识到之前，你已经有几层楼挤满了没有动力去关心的人。

管理一个组织是极其困难的 每个人 是使命驱动的。

> I legitimately don’t understand how companies get to this point

The people who start a company care deeply about the problem they're trying to solve.

The first N employees also care - they're willing to risk working for start-up because they can see the potential and want to help.

But then you hire, say, an accountant. The accountant doesn't care about your mission. You are just another client to them. You need someone to staff the phone or sweep the floors or design the logo or whatever. Why should they care? You're not paying them to care, they're not invested in the company nor its success, and they believe nothing they do for the company will change their personal fortunes.

Then, before you know it, you have a couple of floors filled with people who have no incentive to care.

It is incredibly difficult to run an organisation where everyone is mission driven.

burnt-resistor 2026/03/23 13:08
当领导者允许下属在他们周围建立杜鲁门秀现实扭曲场时，就会发生这种情况，因为这些领导者未能向一线人员或组织不同级别的人员提出问题，与实际客户交谈，并尝试自己使用产品或服务。信任但要验证。领导者永远不能让自己完全脱离完全由下属介导的现实。
It can happen when a leader allows their subordinates to build a Truman Show reality distortion field around them because these leaders fail to ask questions of people on the front lines or in different levels of the organization, talk to actual customers, and try to use the product or service themselves. Trust but verify. Leaders can never allow themselves to be completely isolated from reality mediated entirely by their subordinates.

interludead 2026/03/23 01:19

I think a big part of it is that, at some scale, leaders stop lacking access to reality and start lacking incentive to engage with it
在 HN 中查看
Twirrim 2026/03/22 22:30
大约 10 年前，当最终成为 Oracle 云基础设施的公司刚刚完成第一个可用性域的内部发布时，当我们准备在年底公开发布时，几位高级员工和工程师必须向 Larry Ellison 进行产品演示和演示。

他们进行了一次又一次的试运行，确保演示路径中没有错误。 他们做到了，演示进行得很顺利，现场演示也很顺利。 配置一个裸机实例，让它在启动后几分钟内运行托管某些内容。 拉里对此印象深刻，但最让他印象深刻的是他看到了端到端的现场演示。 参与其中的任何人都从未想过以其他方式来做这件事，但显然，他经常看到的只是产品团队的幻灯片，特别是当产品距离公开发布还有几个月的时候。

我时不时地反思这种情况，想知道在哪个阶段你会从期望看到现场演示转向幻灯片放映。 我认为它会慢慢地从你身边溜走，一次一个，直到你陷入“假装我们有一个好产品”的境地。

About 10 years ago now, when what would eventually become Oracle Cloud Infrastructure had just done an internal launch of the first availability domain, as we got ourselves read for public launch late in the year, several senior staff and engineers had to go do a presentation and demonstration of the product to Larry Ellison.

They did trial run after trial run, made sure trying to make sure there were no bugs in the demonstration path. They nailed it, presentation went smoothly, live demonstration just worked. Provisioned a bare metal instance, had it running hosting something within minutes of launch. Larry was suitably impressed, but the thing that most impressed him was that he'd been presented with an end-to-end live demonstration. It had never occurred to any of the folks involved to do it any other way, but apparently all too often, all he ever saw was slide shows from product teams, particularly when things were several months away from public launch.

I reflect on that situation from time to time, wondering at which stage you sort of go from expecting to see live demonstrations, to slide shows. I assume it just slowly slips away from you, one at a time until you're stuck in the land of "make believe we have a good product".

在 HN 中查看
Quarrelsome 2026/03/22 23:36
> 我时不时地反思这种情况，想知道您在哪个阶段从期望看到现场演示转向幻灯片放映。我认为它会慢慢地从你身边溜走，一次一个，直到你陷入“假装我们有一个好产品”的境地。

我曾经在一个非常小的组织工作，首席执行官有 80 多岁。我觉得有一半的时间幻灯片没有变化，几乎没有完成任何工作，但我不确定首席执行官是否注意到了。鉴于首席执行官经常自己回避这些会议，举办一些奇怪的高级时刻。因此，本来应该是 30m 的会议总是变成了 2 小时以上的会议。这个人曾经很擅长技术，但正如你所描述的那样，他很久以前就失去了技术，而且他未能有效地进行授权。

这些会议感觉就像我真正的工作只是在某种准疗养院的舞台上为老板表演，我们帮助他们维持他们正在经营一家完全正常运作的企业的错觉。我有点想知道，在我短暂的时间里，我是否真的无法完成任何工作。

> I reflect on that situation from time to time, wondering at which stage you sort of go from expecting to see live demonstrations, to slide shows. I assume it just slowly slips away from you, one at a time until you're stuck in the land of "make believe we have a good product".

I used to work a very small org with an 80+ year old CEO. I feel like half the time the slide decks were unchanged and very little was getting done but I'm not sure the CEO noticed. Given the CEO often sidelined those meetings themselves, by having some bizarre senior moments. So what should be a 30m meeting always became a 2hr+ long one. The guy used to be technical but as you describe it had slipped away from him a long time ago and he had failed to delegate effectively.

Those meetings felt like my real job was just theatre for the boss in some sort of quasi-nursing home stage, where we helped them maintain the illusion they were running an entirely functioning business. I half wonder if in my short time there, I was a mug for actually doing any work.

crossroadsguy 2026/03/23 00:14
引用的段落的后半部分以一个可能的反问句结束。

> ... 上述公司的首席执行官最后一次拨打自己的客户支持热线是什么时候？

所以贝佐斯肯定已经很长时间没有这样做了。绝对不是在印度。所以我想说答案应该是：首席执行官这样做或者 必须 只有在公司变得太大或占据了足够根深蒂固的市场份额时才这样做。

The later part of that quoted passage ends with a possibly rhetorical question.

> ... When was the last time the CEO of the above company called their own customer support line?

So Bezos definitely hasn't done that in a long time. Definitely not in India. So I would say the answer ought to be: a CEO does that or has to do that only until the company becomes too big or has captured a sufficiently entrenched large slice of the market.

At one meeting to build out a new service as a next generation to a flagship AWS service that I worked on, I got to meet all the product leaders and managers.

At that meeting, I realized most of them had never used the product and see their claim to leadership role due to their the ability to manage up and down.

I use the product on my personal projects and I hated it with a passion.

在 HN 中查看
skeeter2020 2026/03/22 22:36
在我的上一个角色（工程方面）中，产品副总裁反对我使用“吃你自己的狗粮”这句话，因为它太“恶心”了，她总是插话并用“喝你自己的香槟”代替。我（私下）反驳说这是一个功能；它应该有点倒胃口，因为你来得早，建立同理心并获得不同的视角（对于狗来说，狗粮很美味！）。我认为观点上的差异简洁地说明了试图尽快了解客户体验与数据驱动的 kpi 群体之间的分歧，在这些群体中，很方便的是，你无法发现这些问题，直到为时已晚。
In my last role (engineering side) the VP of Product objected to me using the phrase "eating your own dogfood" because it was "gross" and she always interjected and replaced it with "drink your own champagne". I countered (privately) that was a feature; it's supposed to be a little unappetizing because you're early, building empathy and getting a different perspective (to a dog, dog food is delicious!). I think the difference in perspective succinctly illustrates the schism between trying to understand the customer experience asap, and the data-driven, kpi crowd where - conveniently - you can't discover these issues until it's too late.
在 HN 中查看
geoffschmidt 2026/03/22 23:45
我认为起源在于“狗会吃狗粮吗？”这句话。这是 90 年代和 00 年代常见的风险投资用语，指的是曾经在电视上播放的狗粮广告，意思是“这在内部 Powerpoint 演示中听起来很棒，但客户真的会喜欢它吗？”

归功于 80 年代的一位 Microsoft 高管： https://www.geekwire.com/2025/eat-your-own-dog-food-how-micr...

2015 年，马克·安德森 (Marc Andreessen) 在谈到 Mixpanel 以产品为主导的增长方面取得的成功时，留下了令人印象深刻的一句话：“狗他妈的都跳进纱门去吃狗粮了。而且他还没有做任何营销。” https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2015/05/18/tomorrows-adva...

这就产生了“吃自己的狗粮”的想法，因为如果连你自己都不吃，你说其他狗会吃又有什么可信度呢？

I think the origin is in the phrase, "Will the dogs eat the dog food?" which was common VC-speak in the 90's and 00's, referencing dog food commercials that once ran on TV, and meaning something like "this has been made to sound great in an internal powerpoint presentation, but will customers actually like it?"

Attributed to a Microsoft exec in the 80s: https://www.geekwire.com/2025/eat-your-own-dog-food-how-micr...

In 2015, Marc Andreessen memorably said of Mixpanel's success at product-led growth: "The dogs are fucking jumping through the screen door to eat the dog food. And he hasn’t done any marketing yet." https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2015/05/18/tomorrows-adva...

That then led to the idea of "eating your own dog food", because if even you won't eat it, what credibility do you have saying that the other dogs will?

在 HN 中查看
saltcured 2026/03/23 00:22
我什至不记得我第一次在CS中听到这个表达是什么时候。感觉这在我90年代初上大学的时候就已经是一个成语了。它还 毛毡 就像它利用了我在加州长大时已经拥有的一般文化背景。这不需要任何解释。

虽然无法引用特定的电视广告或其他都市传说的基线，但它清楚地传达了您对自己和产品的更高标准。作为狗粮生产商，您不仅要满足合法销售的最低要求，还要将其做得足够好以适合人类消费。

这与有人证明他们可以安全地饮用或呼吸其他工业过程的副产品属于同一类别。而且，具有讽刺意味的是，还有一个广泛理解的推论，即我们可以期望公关类型做这样的事情，同时暗地里担心这实际上会伤害他们。

I can't even remember when I first heard this expression in CS. It feels like it was already an idiom when I was in university in the early 90s. It also felt like it was tapping into a general cultural background I already had growing up in California. It did not require any explanation.

Without being able to cite a specific TV ad or other urban legend sort of baseline, it clearly communicated that you hold yourself and your products to a higher standard. As a dog-food producer, you don't just meet the minimum requirements for legal sales, but you make it well enough to be fit for human consumption too.

It's in the same category as someone demonstrating that they could safely drink or breathe byproducts of some other industrial process. And, ironically, there was also a widely understood corollary that we could expect PR types to do something like this while secretly fearing that it would actually harm them.

Yeah, I've never really been at all offended by the "eating your own dogfood" phrase, because I always saw that as being the point also.

一般人在看到我时通常显得不太中立。

许多人不仅公开表示敌意，他们还特意立即让你知道他们不是来帮忙的，他们是来制造麻烦的。 每个人的 生活不太愉快。

与人相处时，在很多情况下，如果你不合规矩、不同意，那就这样了。你完成了。他们永远不会认为你值得任何合理的治疗。

The average person generally seems less than neutral to see me.

Many people aren’t just openly hostile, they make a point to immediately let you know they aren’t here to help, they’re here to make everyone’s life less pleasant.

With people, there are many scenarios where if you’re out of line, disagree, that’s it. You’re done. They’ll never ever consider you worth any reasonable sort of treatment.

Dogs, by comparison, are angels.

reverence23 2026/03/22 22:54
>“吃自己的狗粮”，因为它“恶心”

有趣的观点。我在 YouTube 频道上看到一位猎人经常为自己和他的狗做同样的饭菜，甚至用他做饭的煎锅喂他的狗。这有很多实际原因，但狗首先是狩猎和获取食物的主要工具。

> "eating your own dogfood" because it was "gross

Interesting perspective. I watch a YouTube channel of a hunter who routinely cooks the same meal for himself and his dog, and even feeds his dog from the skillet where he cooked the meal. Many practical reasons for that but also the dog being the main tool in hunting and getting that food in the first place.

在 HN 中查看
manarth 2026/03/22 23:23 

    > "a hunter who routinely cooks the same meal for himself and his dog"
“Dogfood”用于区别“食物”、“牛排”、“肉类”等。

您所描述的 YouTube 频道显示的是一只狗被喂食典型的煮熟的“人类”食物，而不是人类吃“狗”食物。

    > "a hunter who routinely cooks the same meal for himself and his dog"
"Dogfood" is used to differentiate from "food", "steak", "meat", etc.

The youtube channel you describe is showing a dog being fed stereotypical cooked "human" food, rather than a human eating "dog" food.

在 HN 中查看
reverence23 2026/03/22 23:35
谢谢您的澄清，英语不是我的母语。最后我猜不同的狗得到的食物质量不同。
Thank you for clarifying, English is not my native language. In the end I guess different dogs get different quality food.
在 HN 中查看
speff 2026/03/22 23:53
据我了解，这通常是出于健康目的（尽管方便也无害）。我的兽医提供的一个例子是，人类所需的钠消耗水平对狗来说太高了。
From my understanding, it's generally for health purposes (though the convenience doesn't hurt). An example my vet provided is that the level of sodium consumption needed by humans is way too high for dogs.
在 HN 中查看
manarth 2026/03/23 00:04
这是有经济效益的：在英国（和许多其他国家），不“适合人类消费”（否则可能会被丢弃）的成分可以加工成宠物食品。

许多商业狗粮都是用不适合人类食用的成分制成的。

There's an economic benefit: in the UK (and many other countries), ingredients which are not "fit for human consumption" (and might otherwise be thrown away) can be processed into pet food.

Much commercial dog-food is made with ingredients which aren't fit to be consumed by humans.

There was a BBC early-morning breakfast tv slot in which a dog was presented as 'vegetarian', until test time...the dog (given free choice between two bowls of food placed on the studio floor) went immmediiately for the meat dish.

在 HN 中查看
carefulfungi 2026/03/22 22:51
有趣的。我更喜欢“香槟”，因为它明确了目标：制作一些精心策划、精心制作和想要的东西。我从来没有把狗粮和香槟的区别解释为某种反同理心。
Interesting. I prefer "champagne" as it clarifies the goal: to make something curated, crafted, and desired. I've never interpreted the dogfood v. champagne difference as anti-empathy somehow.
在 HN 中查看
mcmcmc 2026/03/22 23:20
> 目标：做出一些经过策划、精心制作、令人向往的东西。

这些都不是目标。

目标是交付价值。这句话的意思只是对你自己的产品进行抽样，暗示你应该进行某种形式的质量管理。它也可以很容易地“玩你自己的小部件”。

将其放入香槟中会破坏其大部分意义，因为产品是什么并不重要，无论它多么令人厌恶或与您的个人兴趣无关，您都应该品尝它。让人们为工作而品尝香槟并不困难。

> the goal: to make something curated, crafted, and desired.

None of these are the goal.

The goal is to deliver value. The saying just means to sample your own product, with the implication being that you should be doing some form of quality management. It could just as easily be “play with your own widgets”.

Bougie-fying it to champagne destroys much of the meaning because it literally doesn’t matter what the product is, you should be sampling it no matter how distasteful or irrelevant to your personal interests. You would not have a hard time getting people to sample champagne for their job.

在 HN 中查看
roncesvalles 2026/03/23 01:20
关键是您正在测试的东西不是您的目标用户。香槟是一个坏例子，因为香槟制造商自己肯定会饮用自己的产品，事实上他们可能是鉴赏家。制造您想要的产品是一个非常不同的开发过程 不是 目标客户。
The point is that you're testing something for which you aren't the target user. Champagne is a bad example because champagne manufacturers most certainly themselves imbibe in their own product, in fact they're probably connoisseurs. It's a very different development process to make a product for which you aren't a target customer.
在 HN 中查看
Jeff_Brown 2026/03/22 23:32
Dogfoodung 的意思是放下架子、弄脏自己、把脸放进去。我认为这是完美的。
Dogfoodung usefully connotes getting off your high horse, getting dirty, getting your face in it. I think it's perfect.
在 HN 中查看
justonceokay 2026/03/23 00:07
我试图向自己解释为什么称其为香槟感觉像是自欺欺人。原因是对于 SE，所有软件都是损坏的、有缺陷的、缓慢的、不完整的、具有错误的功能集并且不可扩展。对我们来说，当软件不再让我们出冷汗时，它就被交付了。

对于产品经理来说，假设产品会变成香槟，这感觉非常天真。

I was trying to articulate to myself why calling it champagne feels like self-deception. And the reason is that to a SE all software is broken, buggy, slow, incomplete, has the wrong feature set, and is not extensible. To us software gets shipped when it stops giving us cold sweats.

For a PM to assume that the product ever becomes champagne feels very naive.

dataflow 2026/03/23 00:20
> 获得不同的视角（对于狗来说，狗粮很美味！）

你这不是自毁了自己的论点吗？如果狗粮对狗来说比对人类来说更美味，那么吃它怎么能表明它是否适合狗呢？难道你不应该让你的狗吃它而不是你自己吗？

> getting a different perspective (to a dog, dog food is delicious!)

So isn't your manager's alternative the one that actually makes sense?

在 HN 中查看
omoikane 2026/03/23 00:08
> 喝你自己的香槟

这是一个很好的替代，而且更准确，因为人类和狗对食物有不同的偏好，因此在实践中通常避免人类吃真正的狗粮。

但把“喝你自己的香槟”变成像“dogfooding”这样的动名词就更难了。

> drink your own champagne

This is a great substitution, and more accurate since humans and dogs have different preferences in food, so humans eating real dogfood is often avoided in practice.

But it's harder to to turn "drink your own champagne" into a gerund like "dogfooding".

在 HN 中查看
pinkmuffinere 2026/03/23 00:16
我一直认为“吃你自己的狗粮”是“吃你自己的狗屎”的礼貌化，哈哈
I always assumed that “eat your own dog food” was a polite-ization of “eat your own dog shit”, lol
在 HN 中查看
_doctor_love 2026/03/23 02:41
读了这个故事和相关评论，我很惊讶人们已经失去了有关狗食的历史。据我了解，这个术语实际上是从保罗·马里茨那里学来的。

俗话说：“如果你不吃自己的狗粮，那么你就是在吸自己的毒品。”

非常基本的想法：您的产品是否足够好，您会根据自己的需求和目的使用/消费它？不？如果你认为自己有一个好产品，那么你就是在欺骗自己。

就这么简单。

Reading this story and related comments, I'm struck folks have lost the history around dogfooding. From what I understand the term really picked up from Paul Maritz.

The saying was - "if you're not eating your own dogfood, you're smoking your own dope."

Very basic idea: is your product good enough that you would use it / consume it for your own needs and purposes? No? Then you are deluding yourself if you think you have a good product.

Simple as that.

cainxinth 2026/03/22 21:54
我是那种认为客户服务会很糟糕的人。每当我需要给一家公司打电话时，我都会做好准备，耐心地应对他们的挑战，因为我知道秘诀就是比他们坚持得更久。每次你打电话给他们都要花钱。我经常告诉他们我知道这一点，并向他们保证我会继续打电话，直到问题得到解决。这并不有趣，事情就是这样。

而我老人家，花着自己辛苦赚来的钱，却觉得自己没有得到应有的回报时，还是会感到某种义愤。他喜欢向那些虐待他的公司表达自己的想法，他总是说“我希望我的评论被记录下来以保证质量！”

I’m the kind of person that just assumes customer service is going to be bad. I gird myself whenever I have to call a company and just deal with their gauntlet with patience, knowing the trick is to outlast them. It costs them money every time you call. I’ll often tell them I know that and assure them I will continue calling until the matter is resolved. It’s not fun, it’s just the way things sadly are.

My old man, however, still feels some kind of righteous indignation when he spends his hard earned money and doesn’t feel he’s getting what he paid for. He loves to give a piece of his mind to the companies that mistreat him, and he always says “And I hope my comments are being recorded for quality assurance!”

I had to hold for 2.5 hours, but I spent it catching up with my dad and then my phone rang when the CSA was ready for me. I was so fresh and bright on the call I got an upgrade pretty much just for not being a bitch.

我和其他人一样感到沮丧，但这不是接我电话的人的错。

我记得有一次与康卡斯特通电话时，我只是解释了情况，并开玩笑地说，看，如果有帮助，请随时告诉他们我在大喊大叫。那家伙笑了。一小时后，一位工程师打电话给我，告诉我调制解调器的固件更新。

schiffern 2026/03/22 23:04
”吃自己的狗粮，闻自己的屁“足够粗俗，人们实际上会记住并重述它。作者的比较模因部门的出色工作。

“吃自己的狗粮”==体验自己的产品。

“闻自己的屁”==体验你的 全部的 产品，包括通常不宜提及的内容，例如客户服务和计费

"Eat your own dog-food and smell your own farts" is just crass enough that people will actually remember and retell it. Nice work by the author's Comparative Memetics Division.

"Eat your own dog food" == experience your own product.

"Smell your own farts" == experience your entire product, including things that are typically unmentionables like customer service and billing

在 HN 中查看
derriz 2026/03/22 23:26
这句话很引人注目，但对我来说并没有多大意义。

公司生产狗粮并出售，并期望其他人（狗，它们不会用语言表达对食物的不满）食用它。 狗粮的生产者并不真正关心它的味道或营养价值。

但我无法想象一个涉及收集屁并将其出售给其他人的业务，并且嗅闻每个瓶装屁的少量将有助于改进生产流程，以确保为客户提供更好的体验。 大多数人对别人的屁味感到震惊，但可以忍受自己的屁味，因此“闻自己的屁味”测试并不能真正告诉你任何信息。

The phrase is striking but doesn't make a whole lot of sense to me.

Companies make dogfood and sell it and expect others (dogs, who aren't known for verbalizing dissatisfaction with their food) to consume it. The producers of dogfood don't really care what it tastes like or how nutritious it is.

But I can't imaging a business which involves collecting farts and selling them to others and where sniffing a small quantity of each bottled fart would help improve production processes to ensure a better experience for the customer. And most people are appalled at the smell of others' farts but can tolerate their own, so the "smell your own" test wouldn't really tell you anything.

在 HN 中查看
schiffern 2026/03/23 01:27 

  >The producers of dogfood don't really care what it tastes like or how nutritious it is.
宾果游戏。这就是为什么“吃自己的狗粮”如此有效。最好的公案和寓言都包含矛盾，这是一种教学工具，而不是逻辑错误。

您可以 思考 你不需要吃自己的狗粮（就像狗粮公司一样），但实际上你仍然应该这样做。

  >The producers of dogfood don't really care what it tastes like or how nutritious it is.
Bingo. This is why "eat your own dog food" works so well. The best koans and parables include a contradiction, which is a teaching tool not a logic bug.

You may think you don't need to eat your own dog food (just like the dog food companies), but actually you should still do it.

mock-possum 2026/03/22 23:47
它在这里不起作用，因为“闻你自己的屁”已经是一个习语，意思是“相信你自己的废话”——也就是妄想。

吃自己的狗粮是值得称赞的，而闻自己的屁则不然。

It doesn’t work here because “sniff your own farts” is already an idiom that means “believe your own bullshit” - aka delusional.

Eating your own dogfood is laudable - sniffing your own farts is not.

在 HN 中查看
piker 2026/03/22 23:12
Dogfooding 的问题在于，您必须在许多领域强制执行它，如果您不小心，它可能会推动您解决用户实际上没有遇到的问题。

我们发现自己正试图在氚上找到这种平衡。它是面向律师的文字处理程序，因此具有特定的狭窄领域，使我们能够提供与 Word 不同的体验。但如果我们尝试像 Word 一样使用它，我们最终会想要不符合该策略的通用功能。我写了一些关于我们在这里想到的内容： https://tritium.legal/blog/eat。

这是某些 B2B SAAS 领域中闭源/商业软件的令人信服的理由之一。似乎你无法从 FOSS 的 QA 角度充分测试快乐和悲伤的路径，除非它 (1) 非常成功或 (2) 一个开发工具。

The thing about dogfooding is that you have to force it in many domains, and if you aren't careful it can push you towards solving issues your users don't actually have.

We've found ourselves trying to find this balance on Tritium. It's a word processor for lawyers, so has a specific narrow domain that allows us to provide a differentiated experience from Word. But if we try to use it like Word, we end up wanting generalized features that don't fit that strategy. I wrote a little about what we've come up with here: https://tritium.legal/blog/eat.

This is one of the compelling rationales for closed-source / commercial software in certain B2B SAAS domains. It seems like you just cannot adequately test the happy and sad paths from a QA perspective in FOSS unless it's (1) insanely successful or (2) a dev tool.

在 HN 中查看
sauercrowd 2026/03/22 23:03
这很好地描述了小公司相对于大公司的巨大优势。

小公司的（积极主动的）员工会“关心”，我的意思是他们有责任感，并且可以看到足够多的客户体验，如果出现问题，他们会看到痛苦并尝试解决它。

在大公司没人关心。他们当然关心自己的工作，但他们的工作只占整体客户体验的一小部分，因此很难看到他们的工作对客户产生影响。

这就是为什么大公司需要将客户反馈编码到系统中以模仿反馈周期。主要是在指标方面。 这是一种非常有损耗的捕获信号的方式，并且还有很多不足之处，但到目前为止似乎没有人提出更好的系统。

This describes quite well the huge advantage small companies have vs big companies.

(Motivated) people at small companies "care", and what I mean with that is they are responsible and can see a large enough portion of the customer experience that - if something is broken - they'll see the pain and try to address it.

At a big company no one cares. They of course care about their job, but their job is such a small fraction of the overall customer experience, that seeing their work having an impact on their customer is exceptionally difficult.

That's why large companies need to encode customer feedback into a system to imitate feedback cycles. Mostly in metrics. That's a very lossy way to capture signal, and leaves a lot to be desired, but so far it doesnt seem like anyone has come up with a better system.

在 HN 中查看
TulliusCicero 2026/03/23 02:19
> 这就是为什么大公司需要将客户反馈编码到系统中以模仿反馈周期。主要是在指标方面。这是一种非常有损耗的捕获信号的方式，并且还有很多不足之处，但到目前为止似乎没有人提出更好的系统。

您可以做的另一件事是让高层领导偶尔亲自尝试该产品并直接与客户（尤其是有问题的客户）交谈。

通常，由于官僚主义障碍或不同领地之间的纠纷，问题依然存在：有一个功能需要 X 团队和 Y 团队改进，但这只会对 X 团队的内部指标有所帮助，所以 Y 团队根本不在乎，拖拖拉拉。如果级别足够高的领导者了解并关心这些问题，他们就可以解决这些问题。

> That's why large companies need to encode customer feedback into a system to imitate feedback cycles. Mostly in metrics. That's a very lossy way to capture signal, and leaves a lot to be desired, but so far it doesnt seem like anyone has come up with a better system.

The other thing you can do is having senior leadership occasionally try the product themselves and talk directly to customers (especially ones that have problems).

Often, problems remain because of bureaucratic hurdles, or disputes between different fiefdoms: there's a feature that needs teams X and Y to improve, but it would only help the internal metrics for team X, so team Y doesn't give a shit and drags their feet. Leaders who are sufficiently high in the hierarchy can cut through these sorts of problems if they know and care.

在 HN 中查看
throwaway416430 2026/03/23 01:57
我在一家存在这个主题的小公司管理售后支持，但是，我担心事情可能会朝着错误的方向发展。我需要帮助来处理这种情况。

我们的软件工程团队背负着技术债务和激进的产品路线图的重担。可以理解的是，他们经常觉得有必要推迟并控制事情。当事情进展不顺利时，我们的客户和对客户负责的员工实际上会失眠。我们的工程团队对朝九晚五的工作安排感到沾沾自喜，并在关键问题上睡了好几个星期。

工程团队似乎唯一有动力真正拥有的是升级过程的下一次迭代，这在某种程度上使他们更加不负责任。下一次迭代总是会向 Jira 票证添加更多没人会阅读的详细信息。

基本上该团队中没有人对产品进行测试。损坏的功能正在发货，他们希望我们能够实际销售并支持它。

I manage post sales support at a small company where this theme is present, however, I'm concerned that things may be slipping in the wrong direction. I need help dealing with this situation.

Our software engineering team is burdened with tech debt and aggressive product roadmap. Understandably, they often feel the need to push back and keep things under control. Our customers and the employees who are accountable to our customers literally lose sleep when things aren't going well. Our engineering team feels complacent to function on a 9-5 schedule and sleep on critical issues for weeks.

The only thing the engineering team appears motivated to actually own is the next iteration of the escalation process that somehow makes them even less accountable. Invariably the next iteration includes adding more details to Jira tickets that nobody will read.

Basically no one on that team is dogfooding the product. Broken features are being shipped and they are expecting us to actually sell and support this.

在 HN 中查看
sauercrowd 2026/03/23 20:38
这是我的建议

1. 领导层需要重新构建团队中工程师的角色。这不仅仅是为了建造东西，而是为了帮助客户。工程师的工作是为客户构建一个好的产品，而不仅仅是“构建”、修复 Jiras，...

2.同时，派一些工程师接客户电话。不需要是每个人，但你需要缩短工程师直接从用户那里获得信号的反馈周期。很多信息会通过 Jira、中间体丢失……

他们不需要说话，只需倾听。他们会注意吗？这又回到了他们工作的重构，即为用户创造优质产品。如果每个人都明白这就是目标，他们就会的。

如果您有工程师为您的数据库构建自定义扩展，因为您的 CRM 产品需要一些低级别的性能优化 - 它仍然很好，但不太有趣。

目标是让一些工程师与客户交谈，因为他们会与其他工程师交谈。工程师知道如何与工程师交谈。它将缩短反馈周期，这对速度和信号都有好处。

现在我不知道你已经做了多少，他们甚至可能已经做了所有这些事情，你看到的是已经解决的最糟糕问题的输出。如果是后者，则需要更多的跨团队沟通

Here's what I'd suggest

1. There needs to be reframing of the engineer role in the team, by the leadership. It's not just to build things, but to help the customer. The job of the engineer is to build a good product for the customer, not just to "build", fix Jiras, ...

2. At the same time, get some engineers on customer calls. It doesnt need to be everyone, but you need to shorten the feedback cycle that engineers directly get signal from their users. A lot of information gets lost through Jira's, intermediates, ...

They dont have need talk, just listen. Will they pay attention? That comes back to the reframing of their job being to create a good product for users. If everyone understands that's the goal, they will.

If you have engineers building custom extensions for your database, because your CRM product needs some low level performance optimizations - it'll still be good but less interesting.

The goal is to get some engineers talking to customers, cause they'll talk to other engineers. And engineers know how to talk to engineers. It'll shorten the feedback cycle, and that's good for both speed + signal.

Now I don't know how much of that you already do, and they might even do all these things already, and what you see is the output of the worst issues already taken care of. If the latter is the case, there's some more cross team comms needed

在 HN 中查看
daneel_w 2026/03/22 22:40
对我来说，“dogfooding”并不是像作者所说的那样出于忠诚或一些不伦不类的原则而使用自己的产品。它具体是关于 在开发过程中使用您自己的产品 作为处于最糟糕阶段的一种测试手段——实际上是吃一段时间的狗粮。
To me, "dogfooding" isn't using your own product out of loyalty or some nondescript principle, as the author suggests. It's specifically about using your own product during its development as a means of testing while it's in its worst phase - literally eating dog food for a while.
在 HN 中查看
GuestFAUniverse 2026/03/22 22:18
当建筑师和开发人员不负责戈德堡发明的运行时，这也是同样的马粪。 我想到的另一句话是：没有风险参与。

对我来说，“不负责任”——或者任何更合适的命名——需要它自己的地狱圈。

It's the same horse manure, when architectonauts and developers aren't responsible for the operation of their Goldberg-inventions. Another phrase that comes to mind is: no skin in the game.

To me "unaccountability" -- or whatever naming fits better -- needs its own circle of hell.

mrweasel 2026/03/22 22:06
我曾经接到过两个愚蠢的电话：

1) 我打电话要求取消我出售的汽车的保险单。 IVR 向我打招呼，按三取消保单，我们有了一个良好的开端。接下来是一个长篇演讲，内容涉及如果我被困在中东并需要回家，我需要如何拨打特殊号码，我需要采取的一般预防措施以及我的规则和权利。所有信息都很好，除了我已经表示我打电话取消保单。我坐在巴林的机场，拼命想回家，但我认为现在是检查并取消不需要的保险单的好时机的可能性绝对为零。你已经知道我打电话的原因了，请据此定制消息。

2）互联网已连续第二周中断。客户服务人员正在输入内容，查找内容，试图找出案件结案的原因。 “在我们等待期间，让我和你谈谈我们的流媒体捆绑包”……兄弟，我知道老板让你这样做，但不要试图向你甚至无法上网的客户追加销售流媒体捆绑包。

医生办公室是最糟糕的。他们指导您何时拨打电话以及在哪里拨打电话的整个系统需要几分钟的时间来解释。不管怎样，呼叫都会路由到同一个人。系统中有太多令人困惑和不相关的消息，最终您仍然会被路由到同一组人。

我给客户服务打电话的大部分原因是因为在线自助服务非常糟糕并且无法做简单的事情。如果每个行业的网站稍微好一点，那么他们就可以节省一大笔呼叫中心成本。通常，它甚至与能够自助服务无关，它可能只是提供最微小的实际信息。过去五年你的来电量比平常大，因为你的愚蠢网站一年比一年糟糕。

Two stupid calls I've had:

1) I call to cancel an insurance policy on a car I sold. I'm greeted by the IVR, press three to cancel a policy, we're off to a good start. Next follows a long speech about how I need to call a special number if I stuck in the middle east and need to get back home, general precautions I need to take and my rules and rights. All great information, except I've already indicated that I call to cancel a policy. The chance that I'm sitting in an airport in Bahrain, desperately trying to get home, yet I decide that now is a good time to go through and cancel unneeded insurance policies is absolutely zero. You already know why I'm calling, tailor the message to that.

2) Internet is out, for the second week. Customer service dude is typing in stuff, looking stuff up, trying to figure out why the case has been closed. "While we wait let me talk to you about our streaming bundles"... Dude, I know the boss is making you do this, but don't try to upsell a streaming bundle to a customer you can't even get online.

The doctors office is the worst though. Their entire system for guiding you through when to call and where to call take minutes for them to explain. The call it routed to the same people regardless. There are so many confusing and irrelevant messages from the system and in the end you are still routed to the same set of people.

Most of my calls to customer services is because selfservice online absolutely suck and can't do simple things. Every industry could save a fortune in callcenter costs if their websites was ever so slightly better. Often it's not even about being able to selfservice, it can just be providing the tiniest bit of actual information. Your call volume is larger than normal for the past five years, because your stupid website is getting worse every year.

在 HN 中查看
consp 2026/03/22 22:21
我遇到的最糟糕的情况是，等了大约 10 分钟后，它转到了一个人，他把我推到了另一个部门，结果却出现在我开始通话时使用的相同预选菜单中，但选择了另一个选项，该选项将我带到一个网站，您必须在该网站上输入您的问题，然后收到一封电子邮件，要求致电服务台并选择我首先选择的选项。帮助台生活的圈子。卡夫卡会感到自豪。

这可能是因为他们将两个帮助台合并为一个，使第二部分变得毫无用处，因为他们无法再看到所需的数据，因此完全取消了它，因为它没有获得正确的指标。

事实证明，致电竞争对手服务台更有用，因为他们实际上可以看到我感兴趣的东西，因为它是他们之间共享的（当他们开始挖掘时，暗示他们不打算进行光纤连接工作时间表）。不能使用竞争对手，因为连接是垄断的......

The worst one I've had is that after waiting for about 10 minutes, it passed through to a person who pushed me through the another department, only to end up in the same preselect menu I started the call with but with another option selected which points me to a website where you must enter your question and get an email back with the request to call the service desk and select the option I took in the first place. The circle of help desk life. Kafka would be proud.

This is likely due to them merging two help desks into one making the second part useless as they can no longer see the data they need and thus canceling it entirely since it didn't get the right metrics.

Also turns out that calling the competitors help desk is more useful as they can actually see the thing I was interested in as it is shared between them (fiber connections work schedule when they start to dig, hint they weren't going to). Can't use the competitor since the connections are monopolies...

在 HN 中查看
mrweasel 2026/03/22 22:58
我们在全国大部分地区拥有开放的光纤连接系统。 ISP 相互、电力公司或专用光纤提供商租赁光纤。这意味着您可以在许多区域使用竞争对手状态页面来了解您的连接可能会中断的原因。较大的 ISP 不喜欢被告知您可以看到您所在区域出现断电，而他们自己的监控却都是绿色的。
Here we have an open connect system on fiber in large parts of the country. The ISP lease the fiber each other, power companies or dedicated fiber providers. That means that you can in many areas use the competitors status page to get some idea as to why your connection may be down. The larger ISP do not like being told that you can see that there is an outage in your area, while their own monitoring is all green.

MitchSaid 2026/03/22 22:14
几年前，我被介绍给英国服务咨询公司 Vanguard（及其“Vanguard 方法”），本质上是一种改进服务的系统思维方法（不要与大型美国投资管理公司混淆）。

在他们的世界里，“闻自己的屁”（即倾听，更重要的是，使用规范的学习方法理解对客户来说重要的事情）主要不是同理心，而是获取知识，这样你就可以理解如何干预你的公司作为一个系统。

换句话说，决策者听取客户的电话并不是浪费，事实上，这是他们获得所需知识的唯一途径，以了解如何改进服务（假设这是他们的目标）。

Some years back I was introduced to UK service consultancy Vanguard (and their "Vanguard Method"), essentially a systems thinking approach to improving service (not to be confused with the enormous US investment management company).

In their world, "smelling your own farts" (ie. listening to and, more importantly, understanding what matters to your customers using normative learning methods) isn't primarily about empathy, it's about getting knowledge so you can understand how to intervene in your company as a system.

Put that way, it's not a waste for decision-makers to listen to customer phonecalls, it's in fact the only way for them to gain the knowledge they need to understand what to do to improve their service (assuming that's their goal).

smithcoin 2026/03/23 03:01
我只是假设很多产品经理从未再使用过他们的产品，这在整个行业中都有体现。

您是否曾尝试在长时间未登录后玩 Xbox Live？

您是否尝试过在亚马逊搜索框中使用智能搜索词？

您是否尝试过在 Roku 上使用这些电视频道应用程序？

Fridgeaire 代表拥有自己的电器吗？

我只是确信人们不会使用自己的产品，而使用它们和关心它们的人是如此之少，而且两者之间的距离只能被奉为神圣。

I just assume a lot of product managers have never used their products anymore, and it shows across the industry.

Have you ever tried to play Xbox live after not being signed in for a long time?

Have you ever tried to use smart search terms in the Amazon search box?

Have you ever tried to use these tv channel apps on a Roku?

Do Fridgeaire reps own their own appliances?

I’m just convinced people don’t use their own products where the people that use them and care are so few and far in between things are only getting enshrined.

在 HN 中查看
Frannky 2026/03/22 23:52
我认识一个人。前谷歌。正在做他的创业。花了一年的时间开发一个疯狂的复杂产品。投资者不明白。用户不明白。他99%的时间都在解释为什么他的想法如此好。你要求试用该产品，几周过去了，只有幻灯片和视频演示。当你最终尝试它时，你会发现它是如此令人困惑，而且没有任何效果。我试图让他明白，他需要适应的约束是用户能够快速理解为什么它对他们有用并且它应该起作用。他不在乎。他说这是关于故事的，而这个故事将推动数百万人组建一个超级大团队来实现他的想法。我说这很酷，但为什么同时你不只是选择一件有用且有效的东西，然后在与用户交互并发现更多关于他们的问题的同时程序性地发展到你的愿景。答案是关于谷歌分级系统的一小时演讲。也许他是对的。时间会证明一切。
I know a person. Ex-Googly. Doing his startup. Spent one year on a crazy complex product. Investors do not get it. Users do not get it. He spends 99% of the time explaining why his ideas are so good. You ask to try the product, weeks pass and just slides and video demos. When you eventually try it, it's so confusing and nothing really works. I tried to make him understand that the constraint he needs to fit is users being able to understand rapidly why it's useful to them and that it should work. He does not care. He says it's about the story, and that the story will drive millions unlocking a super big team building his idea. I said that's cool but why in the meantime you don't just go with one thing that is useful and works and then procedurally evolve to your vision while you interact with the user and discover more about their problems. The answer was a one-hour speech on Google leveling system. Maybe he is right. Time will tell.

在 HN 中查看
blfr 2026/03/22 22:31
对于大众产品，我们都受到底部分位数甚至前分位数的支配。这些人需要了解查看网站、收集基本信息等信息。

从医生到店员，每个与普通消费者打交道的人都知道这一点。每个管理这些第一线的人都知道它的成本是多少。因此，队列、提醒、重定向到自助服务。

此外，这也是您可以立即建立自己的能力并受到认真对待的方式。只需从问候语中直接进入基本上下文和您需要的内容，手头有文档，甚至只是加载到您的手机上，等等。

通常还有第二个队列。各种“溢价”优惠（例如更高的银行账户流入量）或只是拥有某人的直接电话号码。

With a mass product we're all at the mercy of the bottom quantile or even vigintile. These are the people who need to hear about checking website, collecting basic information, etc.

Everyone who works with regular consumers, from doctors to shop assistants, knows this. And everyone who manages these first lines knows how much it costs. Hence the queue, the reminders, the redirection to self-service.

Also, this is how you can instantly establish your own competence and be treated seriously. Just go into the basic context and what you need straight from the hello, have documents at hand, even just loaded on your phone, etc.

There's usually also a second queue. Various "premium" offers (like higher inflows bank account) or just having someone's direct phone number.

在 HN 中查看
herodotus 2026/03/22 22:48
并撒谎说：“请仔细听，因为我们的菜单项已更改”。只有一次我得到了每个人都应该使用的东西：“请仔细聆听，以确保您做出正确的选择”。 （说谎也可以通过“我已阅读并同意......”来规范化。也许更好的是：“通过单击此框，您同意我们不必要的冗长条款，无论您是否已阅读它们。”
And lying: "Please listen carefully as our menu items have changed". Only once did I get what everyone should use instead: "Please listen carefully to be sure you make the right selection". (Lying is also normalized by "I have read and agree to ....". Maybe better would be: "By clicking this box, you are agreeing to our needlessly lengthy terms, whether or not you have read them."

chanux 2026/03/22 22:32
每当我需要呼叫 IVR 系统寻求支持时，100 次中有 98 次我都会感到愤怒。这个空间，就是终极暗纹雷区。

我经常与银行和电信公司打交道。绝对垃圾。

我很确定某些系统允许记住 DTMF 菜单并在播放录音时按下它。但我最近调用的系统不允许这样做。就好像他们故意让人等着受折磨一样。

人们称这些系统为最后的手段（至少我这么认为）。让它们变得如此糟糕应该是非法的。

另外，我曾经与这些系统的电信方人员一起工作，他们对这些“功能”感到非常自豪。

抱歉胡言乱语。我不得不发泄出来。

Whenever I have to call an IVR system for support it puts me in a blood rage 98 out of 100 times. This space is the ultimate dark pattern mine field.

My common interactions were with banks and telco companies. Absolute trash.

I'm pretty sure some systems allowed remembering the DTMF menu and press it while the voice recording played. But the recent systems I called did not allow this. It was like they intentionally made people wait to suffer the torture.

People call these systems as a last resort (At least I do). It should be illegal to make them so bad.

Also, I used to work with Telco side guys of these systems and they were very proud of these "capabilities".

Sorry for the rant. I had to vent it out.

在 HN 中查看
shevy-java 2026/03/22 22:40
客户服务有不同的含义。我个人更喜欢 良好、简单且有效的文档。我讨厌必须使用 电话解释问题。亲自做是可以的，但是 这不是我喜欢的方式。

> 当你自己的产品工作时体验它是很好的，但是上述组织中的任何人最后一次经历“困难”的客户旅程是什么时候？

我更喜欢那些制造出从未有过的产品的公司 需要任何东西。甚至没有保修电话，因为事情只是 继续工作。

过去几年我注意到我们过于依赖 在谷歌搜索上。现在很糟糕，寻找高质量的信息 变得更加困难——人工智能趋势正在进一步破坏这一点，因为每个人 现在只有人工智能总结东西，但并不总是有效。

Customer service has different meanings. Personally I prefer good, simple and effective documentation. I hate having to use a phone to explain problems. It's ok to do it in person, but not my preferred way.

> It's all very well to experience your own product when it is working, but when was the last time anyone in the above organisation went through a "difficult" customer journey.

I kind of prefer companies that build products that never ever need anything. Not even warranty calls, because the thing just keeps on working.

What I noticed in the last few years was that we are too dependent on google search. Now that it sucks, finding high quality information has become harder - and AI trend is further ruining this, as everyone just has the AI summarize stuff now, which does not always work either.

在 HN 中查看
joemazerino 2026/03/22 22:34
老问题了。客户服务不应该是令人麻木且深不可测的，响应客户应该很容易。不幸的是，除非高层管理人员参与（是贝佐斯还是盖茨？我都听说过），事情就会在混乱中迷失方向。
The age old problem. Customer service isn't supposed to be mind numbingly unfathomable, and responding to customers should be easy. Unfortunately, unless higher management engages (is that Bezos or Gates? I've heard both) things will get lost in the mix.

best HN headline of 2026
在 HN 中查看
mpalmer 2026/03/22 21:35
Dogfooding 对于用户和服务提供商来说都是一个良性循环，因为激励措施是一致的。

然后生长——对不起，转移——出现了。

感谢转移——对不起，是enshitification——我们已经不再需要进行dogfooding了。我们将其用作一种用户体验陀螺仪，无需过多的机构思考或努力即可使我们保持平衡。它使我们在竞争中更有效率。既然最大的公司受到竞争的威胁最小，他们为什么要承受用户的侮辱呢？

Dogfooding was a virtuous cycle for user and service provider alike, because incentives were aligned.

Then growth - excuse me, metastasis - came along.

Thanks to metastasis - excuse me, enshittification - we've outgrown dogfooding. We'd used it as a kind of UX gyroscope, something that works to keep us balanced without too much institutional thought or effort. It made us more efficient at competing. Now that the biggest firms are the least threatened by competition, why would they subject themselves to the indignities of the User?
